merely grants a license that can be revoked.

paragraphs.text: I strongly welcome the Commissions initiative to introduce a Digital Fairness Act. The Act is both timely and necessary to protect consumers in the evolving digital marketplace, particularly given the growing prevalence of manipulative design practices in the gaming and online entertainment industries. Firstly, as I have experienced first-hand and debated with plenty of others close to me, dark patterns have become a structural problem in mobile and PC gaming. Mobile games such as Candy Crush and Monopoly Go use exploitative design techniques including: Play by appointment mechanics that keep users tethered to the game through artificial time-gating and social obligation mechanics, referral schemes that resemble pyramid structures. These mechanics are not only manipulative but also addictive, particularly for young and vulnerable consumers, leading to time loss, financial detriment, and in some cases compulsive spending behaviors. Even in leading PC and console titles such as Overwatch 2, Apex Legends, or FIFA, players must purchase in-game currency in bundles that rarely match the actual price of digital items. For example, if a cosmetic skin costs 2,400 coins, the purchase options might only be 1,000, 2,000, or 3,000 coinsleaving players with leftover currency that pressures them into additional purchases due to the sunk-cost-fallacy. Additionally, another major fairness issue is the erosion of consumer ownership rights in digital purchases. Even when players buy a physical copy of a PC or console game, it often merely grants a license that can be revoked. Games are sometimes removed from platforms like Steam after purchase, leaving consumers with no recourse despite significant financial and emotional investment. Although this is partially covered by the " EU Stop killing games citizen initiative", I personally have physical copies of games that I can not play anymore because of abrupt end of support. I understand that consumers can not expect ownership of copyrights or patents, but they should at least be guaranteed durable access to the games they purchase. Finally, I have faced issues concerning dynamic pricing and misleading discounts, which also undermine consumer trust. This is applicable almost everywhere, from everyday online clothing stores to eletronic goods. For example, a game like Hollow Knight may be advertised as 15 but is almost always available at a 50% discount. This effectively makes the real price 7.50 while creating the illusion of constant sales. Similar drip pricing and hidden fees in digital marketplaces force consumers to rely on comparison tools and erode trust in the transparency of digital transactions. This forces me personally to constantly use such comparison tools, or overpay by significant amounts. I understand that there are also people who simply don't know that such tools exist, and end up spending way more money for the same goods and services which I find rather unfair. The Digital Fairness Act presents a critical opportunity to address these problems. Tackling manipulative design, ensuring true transparency in pricing, protecting consumer ownership, and safeguarding vulnerable groupsespecially minorswill not only reduce consumer harm but also restore trust in Europes digital markets. I strongly encourage the Commission to ensure that the upcoming legislation sets clear prohibitions against these practices, backed by effective enforcement. By doing so, the EU can remain as the most ethically sound market place for consumers world wide!
